title: Effect of Impedance Change on Instrumentation
narrationScript: In the last two lessons you watched eddy currents push back on the test coil and change its impedance. The problem is that the change is invisible, and small — for a fine crack, often less than one percent of the coil's total impedance. This lesson follows that change through the instrument until it becomes a signal you can act on. The key is Ohm's law for alternating current. The oscillator drives the coil with a constant alternating current, so the voltage across the coil equals current times impedance. When the part changes the coil's impedance, the coil voltage changes with it, in amplitude and in phase. The balance circuit nulls the large standing voltage, the amplifier boosts the tiny difference, and the phase detector splits the result into horizontal and vertical components that move the dot on the display. Amplitude tells you how big the change is. Phase tells you what kind of change it is. That pair of ideas unlocks the impedance plane in the next lesson.

sections.heading: A Real Change You Cannot See
sections.body:
Walk the chain you built over the last two lessons. Alternating current in the test coil creates a **primary magnetic field**. That field induces **eddy currents** in the conductive part. The eddy currents generate an opposing **secondary field** — and the opposition changes the coil's **impedance**: the coil's effective resistance rises because the part dissipates energy, and its inductive reactance falls because the opposing field reduces the net flux through the coil.

Here is the problem this lesson solves: that impedance change is **real but invisible**. No one can see impedance, and the change is small — for a fine surface crack, often **less than 1%** of the coil's total impedance. The entire job of an eddy current instrument is to convert that tiny change into something an operator can see, measure, and act on: a moving dot, a meter deflection, an alarm.

Every instrument, from a simple conductivity meter to a modern impedance-plane flaw detector, does it with the same chain: **drive the coil, capture the voltage change, subtract the steady part, amplify the difference, and display amplitude and phase**. This lesson walks that chain stage by stage.

sections.heading: Ohm's Law for AC: Impedance Becomes Voltage
sections.body:
The instrument's oscillator drives the test coil with alternating current at the selected test frequency. Treat that current as constant — the standard way to model the instrument's front end — and **Ohm's law for AC** does the rest:

**V = I × Z**

With the current I held steady, the **voltage across the coil is directly proportional to its impedance**. Z is the combination you met last lesson — **Z = √(R² + XL²)**, with **XL = 2πfL** — so anything in the part that disturbs R or XL changes the coil voltage immediately.

That voltage carries **two** pieces of information, not one:

- **Amplitude** — how large the voltage is. It reports the **size** of the impedance change.
- **Phase** — how far the voltage's timing leads or lags the drive signal. It reports the **kind** of change, because different test variables shift R and XL in different proportions.

The coil voltage — not the eddy currents themselves — is the instrument's raw material. Everything downstream exists to protect, enlarge, and translate it.

sections.caption: A crack chokes off the eddy currents, so their opposing field weakens and the coil's impedance vector swings back toward its empty-coil value — both its magnitude and its phase angle change (shift exaggerated for clarity). With constant-current drive, the coil voltage copies the change.

sections.title: What the instrument actually measures
sections.body: An eddy current instrument never measures eddy currents directly — they flow inside the part, out of reach. It measures the **voltage across the test coil**: its **amplitude** and its **phase** relative to the drive signal. Because V = I × Z, that voltage is a faithful copy of coil impedance. Every dot movement, meter deflection, and alarm traces back to one event: **the part changed the coil's impedance.**

sections.heading: The Signal Chain: Oscillator to Display
sections.body:
Follow the signal through the five stages every eddy current instrument shares.

- **Oscillator** — generates a stable alternating current at the test frequency and drives the coil. It also hands a **reference copy** of its signal to the phase detector, so the instrument always knows what zero phase means.
- **Balance circuit (bridge)** — the test coil sits in one arm of a bridge network. With the probe on sound material, the operator presses **balance (null)**: the bridge output drops to essentially zero. The large standing coil voltage is cancelled, so only **departures** from the balanced condition pass onward.
- **Amplifier** — a fraction-of-a-percent impedance change produces a voltage far too small to display. High-gain amplification raises the imbalance signal to a working level; the **gain** control sets how much.
- **Phase detector** — compares the amplified signal against the oscillator's reference and resolves it into **two components, horizontal and vertical**. Together they preserve both the amplitude and the phase angle of the change.
- **Filters and display** — filtering strips electrical noise, then the two components drive the readout: a dot on an impedance-plane screen, a meter needle, a recorded trace, or an alarm gate.

Notice what the chain accomplishes: a change you could never sense by hand emerges as a signal you can see, measure, and document.

sections.caption: Block diagram of an eddy current instrument. The oscillator drives the coil and supplies the phase reference; the balance bridge nulls the standing signal; the amplifier boosts the tiny imbalance; the phase detector splits it into horizontal and vertical components for the display.

sections.heading: Amplitude and Phase: Two Channels of Information
sections.body:
On the display, the dot's excursion from the null point is a vector, and you read it on two channels at once.

- **Amplitude — how big.** The length of the excursion scales with the size of the impedance change: a deeper crack, a larger conductivity shift, or more probe wobble all push the dot farther from the null.
- **Phase — what kind.** The angle of the excursion identifies the **source** of the change. Liftoff, a thickness change, and a surface crack each disturb R and XL in different proportions, so each arrives at its own characteristic angle.

Because the phase detector preserves both, the operator can **separate variables**. The standard setup move: with the instrument balanced on a reference part, use the **phase rotation** control to lay the liftoff response flat — horizontal. Probe wobble then merely slides the dot sideways, while a crack drives it upward at a clearly different angle. You will practice exactly this in the lab lessons.

One discipline follows from all of this: a signal is only meaningful **relative to the setup that produced it**. Same frequency, same probe, same balance, same gain, same rotation — then the same discontinuity produces the same signal, and signals become evidence. That repeatability, verified on reference standards, is what standardization means, and the next lesson builds on it to read the impedance plane in full.

sections.caption: Two states of the display. Balanced on reference material, the dot rests at the null point; over a crack, it sweeps out a signal whose length reports amplitude (size of the change) and whose angle reports phase (kind of change). Liftoff has been rotated horizontal.
Readout mechanisms: how different instruments present the same impedance change.
rows: Amplitude of the imbalance only — a needle or a number
Conductivity meters, coating-thickness gauges, simple crack detectors
Amplitude and phase together as a moving dot
Modern flaw detectors — the display this course standardizes on
Signal amplitude against time or probe position
Automated tube, bar, and wire inspection lines
Go / no-go output when the signal crosses a set threshold

figures.title: Block diagram of an eddy current instrument
Impedance change becomes a voltage change
Null, amplitude, and phase on the display

interactive.title: Walk the Signal Chain
interactive.intro: A crack has just passed under the probe and changed the coil's impedance. Click the stage of the instrument whose job is to split the amplified signal into the horizontal and vertical components that position the dot on the screen.

regions.label: Oscillator
regions.feedback: The oscillator generates the stable AC drive at the test frequency and supplies the phase reference. It feeds the chain — it does not resolve the returning signal into components.

regions.label: Balance bridge
regions.feedback: The bridge cancels the large standing coil signal so only changes pass onward. It creates the imbalance voltage, but it does not split that voltage into display components.

regions.label: Amplifier
regions.feedback: The amplifier boosts the tiny imbalance voltage to a usable level. It raises amplitude — it does not separate the signal into horizontal and vertical parts.

regions.label: Phase detector
regions.feedback: Correct. The phase detector compares the amplified signal with the oscillator's reference and resolves it into horizontal and vertical components — preserving both amplitude and phase angle for the display.

regions.label: Display
regions.feedback: The display only draws what it is given: the dot's position comes from components already produced upstream by the phase detector.

checkQuestions.question: An eddy current instrument never senses eddy currents directly. The quantity its electronics actually process is:
options: The eddy currents themselves, picked up by a sensor embedded in the test part
The change in amplitude and phase of the voltage across the test coil
The heat the eddy currents generate as they flow through the part's resistance
The direct current flowing from the probe into the part
checkQuestions.explanation: Through V = I × Z, the coil voltage mirrors coil impedance. When eddy currents change the coil's resistance and reactance, the coil voltage changes in amplitude and phase — and that voltage change is the only thing the instrument processes.

checkQuestions.question: With the probe resting on sound reference material, the operator presses the balance (null) control. The purpose is to:
options: Increase the oscillator frequency until the dot stops moving
Magnetize the part so the eddy currents flow more strongly
Switch off the amplifier between measurements to save power
Cancel the large standing coil signal so only changes from the part are amplified and displayed
checkQuestions.explanation: A small flaw changes coil impedance by a tiny fraction — often under 1% — so the steady signal must be subtracted first. Balancing drives the bridge output to essentially zero; from then on the instrument amplifies only departures from that null.

checkQuestions.question: An instrument drives its test coil with a constant alternating current. When the coil's impedance increases, the voltage across the coil:
options: Increases in proportion, because V = I × Z
Stays the same, because voltage depends only on the oscillator setting
Drops to zero immediately
Becomes direct current
checkQuestions.explanation: Ohm's law for AC: with the current held constant, the coil voltage is directly proportional to impedance. That proportionality is what lets a voltage-measuring instrument report an impedance change.

checkQuestions.question: Which stage of the instrument resolves the amplified coil signal into the horizontal and vertical components that position the dot on the screen?
options: The oscillator
The balance bridge
The phase detector, working against a reference signal from the oscillator
The alarm gate
checkQuestions.explanation: The phase detector compares the amplified signal with the oscillator's reference and splits it into two components. Those components drive the display's horizontal and vertical axes, preserving both amplitude and phase angle.

checkQuestions.question: Two signals on the display have the same amplitude but arrive at clearly different phase angles. To the operator this means:
options: The instrument is out of calibration and must be returned to the manufacturer
The changes in the part are of different kinds — for example, liftoff versus a crack
The two signals came from identical causes
The test frequency drifted between the two signals
checkQuestions.explanation: Amplitude reports how large a change is; phase angle reports what kind of change produced it. Different variables disturb coil resistance and reactance in different proportions, so they appear at different angles — the basis of separating variables on the impedance plane.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, ECT Level I Theory Course §2.1 (eddy current theory: generation, impedance changes, and effect of change of impedance on instrumentation, §2.1.1–2.1.3)
ASNT SNT-TC-1A (2024), Eddy Current Testing Level I (training outline basis for qualification)
ASTM E1316, Standard Terminology for Nondestructive Examinations (electromagnetic testing terms: impedance, balance/null, phase angle)
